opp.name: NZ Ministry of Defence_Merlin Intake + ERP
opp.next_step: Per the email response from Ryan Challis (Director Procurement Excellence) the call with NZ Ministry of Defence is set for 23rd June 2026. They are evaluating for ERP and Merlin Intake modules from Zycus. Currently running on SAP Ariba & JAEGGER
opp.ais_why:
- Ryan Challis mentioned that they do not have any AI enablement.
- They are kee on Merlin Intake.

The NZ Ministry of Defence has an ongoing focus on modernizing procurement while maintaining strict governance, security, and compliance requirements. Public sector organizations in New Zealand are increasingly exploring AI to improve procurement efficiency, supplier management, contract lifecycle management, spend visibility, and administrative productivity. Due to the Ministry's regulated environment, AI adoption is expected to be measured and governed rather than rapid. AI-powered Source-to-Pay (S2P) capabilities, such as intelligent intake, automated sourcing, contract analysis, and supplier risk management, are likely to be of interest where they meet security, transparency, and government procurement standards.

metrics.narrative: No quantified business case exists. No Salesforce field (Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c, Compelling_Event__c) captures cost savings, cycle-time improvement, compliance risk reduction, or ROI target. The Next Step (23 June) states the Ministry is 'evaluating for ERP and Merlin Intake modules' but does not name the problem being solved or the expected outcome. Without a quantified problem and expected impact, the Ministry may deprioritize the evaluation or the incumbents (SAP Ariba, JAEGGER) may retain the business on inertia. For a government procurement displacing two entrenched vendors, a defensible ROI case (cost per transaction reduction, procurement cycle-time improvement, compliance auditability gain, or headcount efficiency) is mandatory to justify the switching cost and change-management lift. The highest-leverage move is to run a discovery or ROI scoping workshop with Ryan Challis and the operational procurement leads in the next 14 days to quantify the pain and build the business case before the Ministry's evaluation stalls.

champion.narrative: No champion has been identified. Ryan Challis (Director Procurement Excellence) is mapped as the Decision Maker contact role, but there is no evidence of advocacy, internal selling, or access to the Economic Buyer or senior stakeholders. A champion actively coaches Zycus on the internal political landscape, introduces us to the Economic Buyer and evaluation committee, shares competitive intel, and pushes back on competitors on our behalf. Ryan's engagement to date is operational coordination (responding to email, scheduling a call), not advocacy. The deal is single-threaded through Ryan, and if Ryan is removed from the process, stalls, or is overruled by a senior executive or procurement board, the deal is exposed. For a government procurement displacing two incumbents, a champion with senior-level access and political capital is mandatory to navigate the evaluation committee and secure EB sponsorship. The immediate move is to assess Ryan's advocacy potential in the next 14 days: 'Ryan, who else should be in this conversation? Can you introduce us to the Economic Buyer, the IT lead, and the operational business unit owners who will use Merlin Intake daily? And what is your sense of the internal appetite for switching from Ariba and JAEGGER—who is pushing for change, and who is defending the status quo?' If Ryan cannot or will not facilitate those introductions, he is a guide, not a champion, and Zycus must find a senior advocate (CIO, Chief Procurement Officer, or operational pain owner) directly.

competition.narrative: Competing against two entrenched incumbents: SAP Ariba and JAEGGER. The Next Step (23 June) states the Ministry is 'currently running on SAP Ariba & JAEGGER,' meaning both are the status quo, not outside challengers—Zycus is the disruptor. SAP Ariba is a mature, globally dominant procure-to-pay platform with deep ERP integration, extensive reference base, and strong brand in government. JAEGGER is less known globally but is entrenched in the Ministry's stack, suggesting a specialized or regional fit (possibly contract management, supplier management, or a niche procurement workflow). Both incumbents have the advantage of user adoption, existing integration, known pricing, and organizational inertia. The Ministry's willingness to evaluate Zycus suggests dissatisfaction with the current stack (pain unknown, see Identify Pain), but without a quantified ROI case and a clear wedge, the incumbents are likely to retain the business by offering pricing concessions, roadmap enhancements, or 'good enough' improvements. The wedge is Merlin Intake AI: if the Ministry's pain is procurement request intake inefficiency, manual triage, or poor visibility into demand, Merlin Intake's agentic automation is a differentiated capability neither Ariba nor JAEGGER can match. The move is to position Merlin Intake as the centerpiece of the demo and ROI case, quantify the intake-cycle-time and procurement-headcount savings it delivers, and surface a reference customer (ideally government or defense) who switched from Ariba and realized the ROI. Also critical: understand why the Ministry is running BOTH Ariba and JAEGGER—if they are solving different problems, Zycus may need to displace both or integrate with one, which changes the commercials and the complexity case.

blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.full: Losing on capability fit — AI interest is confirmed by product scope (Merlin, Merlin Intake) and the Next Step (23 June) explicitly naming 'Merlin Intake modules' in the evaluation. This signals the Ministry sees AI-driven procurement automation as relevant to their needs, placing them in the 'AI Curious' tier at minimum. However, no AIS field (AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c) is populated, and no call or activity documents the depth of the Ministry's AI appetite, their understanding of agentic automation, or whether AI is a must-have vs a nice-to-have. The fit is promising but unvalidated. The risk is that the Ministry sees Merlin Intake as an add-on rather than the centerpiece, or they underestimate the ROI and default to the incumbents (SAP Ariba, JAEGGER) on pricing or inertia. The move is to validate and deepen the AI appetite by quantifying the Merlin Intake ROI (intake-cycle-time reduction, procurement-headcount efficiency, demand-visibility improvement) and positioning it as the primary reason to switch from Ariba/JAEGGER.

hard.ais_why:
- Ryan Challis mentioned that they do not have any AI enablement.
- They are kee on Merlin Intake.

The NZ Ministry of Defence has an ongoing focus on modernizing procurement while maintaining strict governance, security, and compliance requirements. Public sector organizations in New Zealand are increasingly exploring AI to improve procurement efficiency, supplier management, contract lifecycle management, spend visibility, and administrative productivity. Due to the Ministry's regulated environment, AI adoption is expected to be measured and governed rather than rapid. AI-powered Source-to-Pay (S2P) capabilities, such as intelligent intake, automated sourcing, contract analysis, and supplier risk management, are likely to be of interest where they meet security, transparency, and government procurement standards.
